title: Morning Robotics Scan
report:
## CORAL-AUV: CFD‑informed RL for AUVs

### The Breakthrough
The paper introduces surrogate drag models trained on CFD data that can be queried instantly during reinforcement‑learning, enabling a zero‑shot policy transfer to a real 6‑DOF autonomous underwater vehicle. Using these CFD‑derived surrogates, the learned policy consumes 31% less energy, moves 11% faster, and is 19% more accurate than a controller with simplified physics in tank and field tests.

### The Caveats
The paper compares its CFD‑surrogate RL policy only to a simplified‑physics controller, offering no benchmark against other contemporary RL‑based AUV controllers.

## UN adopts binding framework for automated driving

### The Breakthrough
The UNECE approved the world’s first binding regulation for fully automated driving systems, mandating a safety‑case approach and a unified validation methodology that includes physical test‑tracks, practice runs, and extensive simulation toolchains. Vehicles must be equipped with data recorders to log safety‑critical events, and the rule aligns roughly ninety existing UNECE regulations, covering robotaxis lacking traditional controls.

### The Caveats
The framework is announced without any empirical testing or independent validation, so its real‑world safety impact remains unproven.

## CoDiMAD: Diffusion‑based privileged distillation for multi‑robot coordination

### The Breakthrough
CoDiMAD distills a globally informed MAPPO oracle into decentralized controllers via conditional diffusion models, preserving multimodal action distributions and avoiding the mode‑averaging pitfall of deterministic distillation. Experiments on three cooperative tasks show higher success rates than direct local MARL and deterministic distillation baselines.

### The Caveats
The performance improvements are demonstrated only in simulation on three tasks, with no real‑world robot experiments.

## PAC‑ACT: Post‑training actor‑critic for action‑chunking manipulation

### The Breakthrough
PAC‑ACT builds a chunk‑level actor‑critic on top of a pretrained Action Chunking Transformer and adds a hybrid behavior‑prior constraint, fine‑tuning the policy while staying close to the original distribution. On the Contour benchmark it reduces peak contact force by a factor of 46 and lowers high‑force events, while retaining low inference latency and minimal GPU memory use.

### The Caveats
The paper provides no comparison against existing RL fine‑tuning baselines, leaving the improvement unsubstantiated.

## B‑spline Policy: Continuous action representations for fast manipulation

### The Breakthrough
B‑spline Policy encodes an entire action sequence as a set of knots and control points defining a B‑spline curve, enabling smooth, time‑continuous trajectories that can be temporally scaled. Integrated into standard policy learning pipelines, it achieves a significant speed‑up over unnamed baselines without sacrificing task success rates.

### The Caveats
The speedup is reported against unnamed baselines on ad‑hoc tasks, making it unclear how BSP compares to state‑of‑the‑art methods.

## LingBot‑Video: Open‑source MoE video model for embodied AI

### The Breakthrough
LingBot‑Video employs a scalable mixture‑of‑experts architecture delivering roughly 3× faster inference while training on over 70,000 hours of web and embodied video data. Its two‑stage pipeline rewrites prompts into structured JSON captions, applies an auto‑negative filter, and then generates video via diffusion, achieving top scores on the internal RBench benchmark.

### The Caveats
The SOTA scores rely only on an internal RBench leaderboard against unnamed baselines, without comparison to strong prior methods or ablations of the MoE component.

## franky: ROS‑free high‑level control for Franka arms

### The Breakthrough
franky wraps libfranka in a high‑level Python/C++ API that accepts position or velocity targets and delegates to the Ruckig planner for real‑time, time‑optimal trajectory generation respecting velocity, acceleration, and jerk limits. A pip‑installable package and a drop‑in simulator enable rapid prototyping without ROS dependencies.

### The Caveats
No real‑hardware performance data or latency measurements are provided, so the claim of real‑time, ROS‑free control remains unverified.

## jax_fdm: Auto‑differentiable force density method

### The Breakthrough
The JAX implementation of the classic force density method provides exact gradients, JIT compilation, and parallel execution on CPUs, GPUs, or TPUs, allowing gradient‑based tuning of force densities, loads, and supports within end‑to‑end differentiable models.

### The Caveats
The library’s performance and scalability are unverified, lacking benchmarks against existing FDM implementations or real‑world hardware tests.

## Lightly‑Train: All‑in‑one vision model lifecycle

### The Breakthrough
Lightly‑Train unifies self‑supervised pre‑training (DINOv2/v3), fine‑tuning, and universal distillation, introducing LTDETRv2 which reaches 50.7 mAP on COCO with 5.4 ms latency on 640×640 inputs and supports ONNX/TensorRT export for edge inference.

### The Caveats
The claim that LTDETRv2 will accelerate robot perception is unverified, as evaluation is limited to COCO simulation with no real‑world robotic experiments.

## Claude Code token overhead vs OpenCode

### The Breakthrough
Instrumentation of the API request pipeline revealed that Claude Code sends roughly 33 k tokens before reading the prompt, whereas OpenCode incurs only about 7 k tokens, indicating a five‑fold higher token overhead due to a less efficient caching strategy.

### The Caveats
The analysis reports only raw token counts from a small, unspecified sample and provides no data on coding correctness, latency, or real‑world impact.

### Priority
CORAL‑AUV showcases a concrete bridge between high‑fidelity CFD simulation and real‑world underwater robot control, delivering measurable energy and speed gains that directly matter to robotics practitioners.

### One action
Explore integrating CFD‑derived surrogate drag models into your own RL pipelines to accelerate training and improve real‑world performance of fluid‑dynamic robots.

### What to watch
Recent work on manipulation and coordination is moving toward richer policy representations: PAC‑ACT refines chunk‑level transformers for safer contact, B‑spline Policy replaces discrete steps with continuous splines for speed, and CoDiMAD leverages diffusion to distill multimodal coordination policies. Together they signal a trend of combining advanced function approximators with safety or communication‑free constraints, which could reshape how we design real‑time, collaborative robot controllers.
